---
title: "Chapter 2: Time-Series Simulation and DER Modeling"
bibliography: ../../references.bib
---

Chapter 1 ended on a warning, not a conclusion: a single power-flow solve is
a photograph, not a verdict, and the `.ark-mistake` box there proved it with
one feeder at three different loading levels. But an engineer deciding
whether to approve one more rooftop solar application doesn't get to pick a
loading level. They get a real feeder, a real year of smart-meter readings,
and a question that only a real time series can answer: not "is this
network healthy right now," but "is it healthy enough, on its worst day,
for what's about to be connected to it."

This chapter builds the rest of `ark.dss.Circuit` around exactly that
question. By the end, the same 31-customer AusNet feeder from Chapter 1
runs a full day, half hour by half hour, with real smart-meter demand and
real rooftop solar attached, and answers a real question: how much
{{< acr PV >}} can this specific feeder actually take before it breaks its
own voltage limit.

## Getting the data

Everything below runs on Team-Nando's real AusNet network and smart-meter
data, the same source Chapter 1 introduced. It isn't checked into this
repository (someone else's licensed data, vendored locally instead of
committed), so fetch it once before running the notebook:

```bash
uv run python scripts/fetch_part4_ausnet_data.py
```

That pulls the network model and two real datasets into
`resources/cvar_flexibility/data/timeseries-lv/`: 342 anonymized customers'
active-power readings, and their matched normalized {{< acr PV >}}
generation, both at 30-minute resolution for a full year.

## A year, not a moment

A smart meter's real output is a time series: one active-power reading
every half hour, for as long as the meter has been installed. OpenDSS's
equivalent is a `LoadShape`, an array of readings a `Load` or `PVSystem` can
be told to follow instead of holding one fixed value. Building one from a
real reading is the whole bridge between what Chapter 1 modeled (a network)
and what this chapter runs (a network under real, changing demand).

The notebook's own `build_simple_lv_network()` rebuilds Chapter 1's
three-house network exactly, transformer, backbone, three single-phase
houses, wrapped in a function so every section below can start from a
clean copy rather than one circuit's accumulated state.

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#b2096981 >}}

`load_data` is 342 real customers by 365 days by 48 half-hour readings:
one full year of actual AusNet smart-meter data. `pv_data` is one
normalized generation profile per day, since the sun doesn't run on a
per-customer schedule the way demand does.

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#47ed9cd1 >}}

A meter only measures active power; reactive power (`qmult`) isn't
metered, so it's derived from an assumed power factor, the same 0.95-0.99
range Team-Nando's own tutorials use. `useactual=1` tells OpenDSS the
`pmult`/`qmult` arrays are already in kW/kvar, not multipliers on the
load's rated capacity. From `edit load.Load_1 daily=customer0_profile`
onward, every time-series solve reads `Load_1`'s demand from this real
reading instead of a fixed number.

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#08294d4f >}}

Customer 0's day 45: quiet overnight, a small midday bump, a sharp evening
peak past 3kW. An ordinary residential profile, and now something a
network can actually be driven by.

## Solving across a day

`circuit.solve_daily(steps, stepsize)` is the piece Chapter 1 didn't have:
it sets `Mode=daily` and steps through one solve per interval, the
LoadShape supplying a different reading at each step instead of a value
frozen in time.

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#08fc2bde >}}

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#3a53ec25 >}}

Voltage barely moves, 0.976820 to 0.976823 pu, a swing of about 0.000003
pu, nothing a real meter would ever register. That's a real result, not a
failed demo. Chapter 1's backbone (a 250kVA transformer feeding a short,
heavy three-phase line) is strong relative to one house's ordinary swing
between roughly 0.3kW overnight and 3kW at its evening peak. A network's
strength matters as much as a load's size, and the mechanism this section
demonstrates, solving across time instead of once, is exactly as real even
on a feeder that barely shows it. The hosting-capacity study at the end of
this chapter puts real demand from 31 real customers on a thinner, real
feeder, and there the same mechanism produces a result worth acting on.

## Modeling PV properly

A `PVSystem` can run on a flat rating, constant output regardless of the
time of day, the same way Chapter 1's own exercises added PV. A more
realistic model drives it from an irradiance profile and a temperature
profile instead, a `LoadShape` for sunlight and a `Tshape` for panel
temperature.

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#3f176432 >}}

::: {.ark-mistake}

**A 20% default nearly zeroed out the morning and evening.** OpenDSS's
`PVSystem` defaults `%cutin`/`%cutout` to 20%, the inverter output
threshold below which it won't turn on or stays off. At 10% irradiance
(hour 6 above), a real inverter still produces something, 0.1 x 5kW =
0.5kW, but a 20%-cutin model reports flat `0.0`, and it's easy to miss
unless every hour gets checked individually: hours 12-16, the middle of the
day, look identical either way. Team-Nando's own tutorials always override
this to `%cutin=0.05 %cutout=0.05`.

:::

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#fbac9946 >}}

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#04938480 >}}

Rebuilding the same PV system with the lower threshold recovers exactly the
two hours the default silently dropped, visible directly against the
default in the chart above.

## Mitigation strategies: Volt-Watt and Volt-VAr

A large rooftop system on a weak feeder connection can push local voltage
above the statutory limit long before the transformer itself is stressed.
{{< acr IEEE >}} 1547-2018 standardizes exactly this kind of smart-inverter
response [@ieee1547-2018], and OpenDSS models it through an `InvControl`
element driven by an `XYCurve` of voltage/output setpoints. Volt-Watt
curtails real-power export as voltage rises past a threshold; Volt-VAr
absorbs reactive power instead, trading export for headroom without
necessarily cutting generation at all.

The network below is deliberately a stress case, one house with a 10kVA
system on a long, thin service cable, so the effect is visible without
guesswork about whether it would ever actually trigger.

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#ad28e9ab >}}

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#d80b7647 >}}

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#23b7bbc2 >}}

Volt-Watt cuts this house's export from 10kW to 7.32kW and pulls voltage
back from 1.0887 to 1.0637 pu, a direct, working trade of generation for
headroom.

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#7bb48503 >}}

Volt-VAr only commands reactive power, `Q`, yet real power dropped too,
from 10kW to 8.35kW. The inverter's own `kVA` rating is the reason:
`sqrt(P^2 + Q^2)` is capped at 10kVA, so absorbing 5.5kvar of reactive
power leaves less apparent-power headroom for real power within that same
limit. Volt-Watt and Volt-VAr aren't really two independent levers, they
share one inverter's capacity.

## Storage: the third DER

A `Storage` element is a dispatchable battery: `state=CHARGING` draws power
from the network, `state=DISCHARGING` supplies it, both bounded by
`kwrated` (power) and `kwhrated`/`kwhstored` (energy). Unlike a `Load` or a
`PVSystem`, its output sign flips with its own commanded state, not with
the weather or the time of day.

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#95961602 >}}

The comment in that cell is worth reading as carefully as the code:
OpenDSSDirect.py is a singleton engine, one compiled circuit per process,
so building a second `Circuit` `Clear`s and recompiles the same global
state Chapter 1's own `base_peak_loss_kw` note already warned about. Two
`Circuit` objects can exist in Python at once, but only one of them is ever
telling the truth about what's currently solved. Reading each result
immediately after its own solve avoided a real version of this bug caught
while drafting this notebook, not just a hypothetical one.

## A real PV hosting-capacity study

Every piece above comes together here: real load data becomes LoadShapes
on the real 31-customer AusNet feeder, a chosen share of customers get real
PV profiles, and the network solves across a full day, half hour by half
hour, at increasing PV penetration, the same methodology Team-Nando's own
hosting-capacity tutorials use.

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#c405ad16 >}}

The notebook's own `run_penetration` function allocates one of the 342
real customer profiles to each of the network's 31 loads (more real
customers exist than houses on this feeder, so every run samples a fresh
31), gives a chosen percentage of them a real 5kVA PV system, and records
the worst bus voltage anywhere on the feeder at every half-hour step.

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#d94722a5 >}}

Day 363 is the sunniest day in the dataset, the highest normalized PV
profile of the year, and the worst case for a hosting-capacity study: if a
penetration level is ever going to cause a violation, a low-demand,
high-sun day is where it shows up first.

{{< embed 02-timeseries-der-modeling-code.ipynb#816008f5 >}}

The feeder-wide maximum stays inside the compliant band through 60%
penetration, then crosses the 1.10 pu statutory limit at 80%. That's a real
answer to a real question, not a theoretical ceiling: this specific feeder,
on its sunniest day, with realistically-sized rooftop systems, can host
roughly six in ten customers with solar before it needs an intervention,
Volt-Watt, Volt-VAr, or a network upgrade, to host more. A utility engineer
asking the same question on a different feeder would run this exact
notebook, swap the network files, and get their own number.

## Where Part 4 goes from here

Chapter 1 built the vocabulary: a network, a solve, a per-unit voltage
reading. This chapter turned that into something closer to how a utility
actually studies a feeder, real smart-meter data driving a real time
series, DER models with real physics behind them, and a hosting-capacity
result with a real number attached. Part 4 now follows four threads, each
building on the same `ark.dss.Circuit` this chapter just extended:

1. **Phase detection**, recovering which phase a customer is actually
   connected to from nothing but voltage patterns, a real, common gap in
   utility records.
2. **Customer and feeder clustering**, grouping customers and feeders by
   how they actually behave, not just how they are labeled.
3. **Voltage-violation and power-quality anomaly detection**, catching a
   compliance problem, or a meter reporting bad data, as it happens rather
   than after a complaint call.
4. **Grid health under forecasted {{< acr DER >}} growth**, where Part 3's
   forecasts become the scenario driver for exactly the kind of
   hosting-capacity study this chapter just ran, and mitigation strategies
   like Volt-Watt and Volt-VAr get evaluated against it directly.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
